pending.label: Edda - Letter 6 variant A (lifted the latch, for whoever finds the seat next)
pending.variant:
I have stopped walking.

I am in a small carriage near the back. Or near the front. The light is the same in both directions.

There is a window. There is a latch on the window. I have been looking at the latch for a long while.

The three heads are here. They are not at the glass now. They are a little way out from it, in the dark. Waiting.

I have decided I am not going to walk again. I have decided I am going to lift the latch.

I have lifted the latch.

The window is open. The night is very cold. The cedar smells stronger when the air moves through it.

I am going to leave this here. I am going to leave the window open too. For whoever finds the seat next.

pending.label: Edda - Letter 6 variant B (leaving the window open too)
pending.variant:
I have stopped.

A small carriage. Near the back. Or the front. The light is the same in both directions.

A window. A latch. I have been looking at it a long while.

The three heads are here. Not at the glass now. A little way out. Waiting.

I am not going to walk again. I am going to lift the latch.

I have lifted it.

The window is open. The night is cold. The cedar smells stronger when the air moves.

Leaving this here.

Leaving the window open too.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 41 variant A (Edda Maer interview)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-One.

Witness: Edda Maer. Carriage 114. Long aboard.

Reports: figure in the doorway. Eyes white. Train at full motion. Five seconds. Gone.

Cross-questioned three times. Story did not move. Detail held.

The witness will not retract. Forty entries did. This may be the forty-first.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 41 variant B (the laugh)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-One.

A man across from me asked what I was writing.

I said: field notes.

He said: about what.

I said: the white-eyed man.

He laughed.

It was the laugh of a person who has never seen him. I have catalogued the laugh.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 43 variant A (stubborn witness Pell Cordoba)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-Three.

Witness: Pell Cordoba. Insists.

I offered her: lamp afterimage. Carriage flicker. Eye fatigue from the tunnels.

She said: I have lived underground. I know lamps.

I offered her: a man wearing eye protection. Some mining cults do.

She said: he had no shadow. The lanterns made shadows. He did not.

I closed the entry without an explanation.

This is unusual for me.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 43 variant B (four witnesses, same sentence)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-Three.

Today I have interviewed four. Independent. No cross-contamination.

Carriage 87: white eyes through the door glass.

Carriage 154: white eyes through the door glass.

Carriage 226: white eyes through the door glass.

Carriage 401: white eyes through the door glass.

I have written the same sentence four times.

Four different witnesses wrote it before me.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 48 variant A (Joren Vask, hypnagogic theory)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-Eight.

Witness: Joren Vask. Carriage 519.

Could not finish the interview. Twice put down the pen and stared at the floor.

Three witnesses today have done this. Same posture. Same delay.

Hypothesis: prolonged tunnel exposure produces a recurring hypnagogic figure. The white eyes are the afterimage of carriage lamps on the cone cells.

The trauma is real. The figure is not.

This is the most coherent theory I have constructed in years.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 48 variant B (folie a deux at scale)
pending.variant:
Sighting Forty-Eight.

Working hypothesis.

Sustained tunnel-flicker. Disrupted circadian rhythm. Shared narrative exposure between strangers.

These conditions produce a folie a deux at scale. The white-eyed man is the train's local hallucination. Every long-haul population invents one.

The trauma is real. The figure is shared because the conditions are shared.

This is the entry I have been waiting to write for eleven years.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 61 variant A (clocking the witness)
pending.variant:
Sighting Sixty-One.

I entered Carriage 47. A woman was eating.

I knew before she looked up.

The way she held the spoon. The way her eyes did not move toward the door glass when the train flickered.

I sat down. I said: when did you see him.

She said: yesterday.

The interview took two minutes.

I am very good at this.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 61 variant B (catalogue of signs)
pending.variant:
Sighting Sixty-One.

The signs I now recognise:

The flinch at carriage flicker. The half-second of held breath at a door opening. The avoidance of door glass.

The way they hold their cup with two hands.

I enter a carriage and read the room.

Three witnesses today. None disagreed with my opening question.

Eleven years. This is the year it has all paid off.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 78 variant A (Sora Penn, letting the lie stand)
pending.variant:
Sighting Seventy-Eight.

Witness: Sora Penn. Carriage 488.

Claims she has seen nothing.

She has the spoon-hold. The door-glass avoidance. The half-second of held breath.

She has every sign in my notebook.

I thanked her for her time. I did not contradict her.

She wanted to be left thinking she was fine.

I left her thinking that.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 78 variant B (the quiet, no verb fits)
pending.variant:
Sighting Seventy-Eight.

Some of them are trying to hide it now.

They mention the name without meaning to. They look at door glass and then look away too fast.

One man said the word "shadow" three times in five minutes. Unprompted.

I do not press. I write the entry afterward.

I used to think I was studying them.

I am no longer sure what the verb should be.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 92 variant A (unrecordable, Witness: myself)
pending.variant:
Sighting Ninety-Two.

Witness: myself.

Carriage 612. Door glass. Late.

Something stood. Or did not.

The carriage flickered. The figure flickered with it. Then neither.

This is the kind of entry I have closed in retraction forty times.

I cannot record eye colour. I cannot record duration. I cannot record absence of shadow.

I can record that I stopped walking.

I record the witness response. This is one.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 92 variant B (marginal collapse, brackets)
pending.variant:
Sighting Ninety-Two.

Witness: H. Pent.

[The interviewer is also the witness. This invalidates the entry.]

Two carriages back. Through the door glass. A figure. Eyes white. Five seconds.

[The entry is recorded anyway.]

I cannot trust my own observation. I cannot dismiss it either.

I have spent the night in the corridor.

- H.P.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 140 variant A (methodical archive search)
pending.variant:
Sighting One Hundred & Forty.

Returned to cabin. Opened archive crate.

I am looking for my own sighting in someone else's entry. A precedent.

Cross-ref: Sighting 17. Sighting 22. Sighting 41.

Sighting 17 — witness could not record duration. Could not record eye colour. The match is exact.

It should be a relief.

It is not. The date line is in different ink. I do not remember changing pens.

Probably nothing.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 140 variant B (Sighting 17 quoted excerpt)
pending.variant:
Sighting One Hundred & Forty.

What I saw last week — if I saw anything — should be in here somewhere. A precedent. Some other witness with the same gaps.

I found Sighting 17.

"Witness reports figure in mine shaft. Eyes white. Six seconds. Subject cannot account for the time after."

I do not remember interviewing this person.

The phrasing is mine. The handwriting is mine.

The dates do not line up with my field trips that year.

Probably nothing.

- H.P.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 211 variant A (the dates are wrong)
pending.variant:
Sighting Two Hundred & Eleven.

The dates are wrong.

Sighting 22 is dated three months before I was hired.

Sighting 31 describes the train. I did not know the train existed.

Sighting 41 describes Edda Maer. I met Edda four weeks ago.

I have not slept. The number above this entry is a guess.

The notebook is not what I thought it was.

- H.P.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 211 variant B (notebook contains my future)
pending.variant:
Sighting Two Hundred & Eleven.

I am holding a notebook that contains my future.

Sighting 41, dated nine years ago, reads:

"Witness: Edda Maer. Carriage 114. Long aboard."

I wrote that sentence last week.

[The page has aged.]
[The binding is original.]
[The ink is mine.]

I have stopped knowing how to date these entries.

- H.P.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 240 variant A (interview reversed, dining carriage)
pending.variant:
Sighting Two Hundred & Forty.

A woman sat across from me in the dining carriage.

She asked: have you seen him.

She looked at me the way I have looked at four hundred people.

I said: I do not know what you mean.

It was Sora Penn's line. I wrote it down four weeks ago. I am hearing it in my own voice.

I did not finish the meal. I returned to my cabin. I have not opened the door since.

The notebook is open.

- H.P.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting 240 variant B (catalogued, paranoia)
pending.variant:
Sighting Two Hundred & Forty.

I have been catalogued.

They look at me the way I taught myself to look at them.

The way they hold their cups. The small slow turns toward door glass.

In the corridor today, a man stepped back from me. He had the recognition.

He did not ask. He knew.

I will not write what he knew.

I will not write what I saw.

I am writing about the notes.

- H.

pending.label: Halen - Sighting — variant A (composed acceptance, finale)
pending.variant:
Sighting —

I have stopped numbering.

I went back to the earliest entries.

Sighting 1 is dated thirty-seven years ago.

I am thirty-seven.

Sighting 1 is in my handwriting. The same loop on the y. The same comma habit.

I do not remember being six.

I do not remember being eight, ten, twelve. I remember adult things. I remember nothing before the field.

On the inside of the back cover, in a hand I have never seen, are five words:

"I gave you the notebook."

I have been reading and writing this notebook all of my life. I did not write this line.

I do not know who did.

- H. Pent

pending.label: Halen - Sighting — variant B (fragmented confession, finale)
pending.variant:
Sighting —

[I have stopped numbering.]

The earliest entry. Sighting 1.

The date. Thirty-seven years ago.

I am thirty-seven.

[The handwriting is mine.]

[I do not remember being six.]
[I do not remember being twelve.]
[I do not remember being twenty.]

The first thing I remember is the field. I was already a folklorist. I had already been writing in this notebook.

Inside the back cover, written in a hand I do not know:

"I gave you the notebook."

I have been answering it my whole life.

-

pending.label: Soll Letter 1 variant A (loadout, S. Marra)
pending.variant:
Day One. Loadout.

Iron sword, x2 (one back-up per the notebook).
Bow, 64 arrows.
Enchanted leather, full set, Protection I.
Ender pearls, x16.
Bed, x1.
Raw potatoes, x32.

The notebook calls for gold apples. The town had none. I will source them en route.

The rat is real. I have logged him.

Day One.

- S. Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 1 variant B (loadout, snuck out)
pending.variant:
Day One. Loadout.

Iron sword, x2.
Bow, 64 arrows.
Enchanted leather, Prot I.
Ender pearls, x16.
Bed.
Potatoes, x32.

The notebook says gold apples are non-negotiable. The town had none. I left before anyone noticed I was gone.

A rat in a vest gave me this paper. He looked sad. I do not know what to log him as.

I am ready.

- Soll Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 2 variant A (notebook, bringing it)
pending.variant:
The notebook is Halric's. He started it twenty-six years ago, the year before he met me.

Specs for the End. Pillar heights. Crystal cages. Cage break order. Bed-blast yields. Pearl trajectories.

He never went.

He died in spring. Lung-thing. The notebook calls it "cessation, non-combat."

The dragon never met Halric.

I am bringing it to her.

- S. Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 2 variant B (notebook, bringing him)
pending.variant:
The notebook is Halric's. He started writing it the year before I was born. Twenty-six years of specs and no dragon at the end.

Pillar heights. Crystal cages. Cage break order. Bed-blast yields. Pearl trajectories. The whole fight, on paper.

He died in spring. The notebook calls it "cessation, non-combat." I think it was his lungs. He coughed a lot. He never said the word.

The dragon never met Halric.

I am bringing him to her.

- Soll

pending.label: Soll Letter 3 variant A (drill one, what ready feels like)
pending.variant:
Drill One. Carriage 14.

Target: zombie, no armour.
Range: 9 blocks.
Cover: a bench.
Bow: full draw, two seconds.

Halric's perch-cycle adapts. The carriage lamp stands in for the crystal. Drop the crystal first. Pivot. Loose.

Result: one shot. Notebook target time eleven seconds. Mine, four.

The notebook does not say what ready feels like.

- S. Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 3 variant B (drill one, the lamp swung)
pending.variant:
Drill One. Carriage fourteen.

I picked a zombie because the notebook starts with crystals and I do not have a crystal. The lamp on the wall was the right shape.

Bench cover. Nine blocks. Full draw. Pivot. Loose.

The zombie went down in one. Halric's target time was eleven seconds. I took four.

I was ready, then. Then I sat on the bench and the carriage was quiet and the lamp swung a little.

I do not know what I expected.

- Soll

pending.label: Soll Letter 4 variant A (sighting, fourth apprentice)
pending.variant:
Carriage 28. Encounter.

Passenger: female, mid-fifties, grey wool coat. Apparently real.

She asked my name. I gave Halric's title. Apprentice of Halric.

She said, "Halric. So you're his fourth."

I did not know there had been three.

I noted her seat number. I moved on.

Sighting closed.

- S. Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 4 variant B (witness, fletcher with cough)
pending.variant:
Carriage twenty-eight. A woman in a grey coat asked my name.

I gave Halric's title. Apprentice of Halric.

She said, "Halric? The fletcher with the cough?"

I did not know he was a fletcher. I have one of his bows in my pack.

I should have sat down. I should have asked her what else she knew. I did not. I walked to the next carriage and I am sitting here writing.

She is still in seat 14. I think.

- Soll

pending.label: Soll Letter 5 variant A (eye of ender, train not in a direction)
pending.variant:
Attempt 47. Eye of ender.

Throw vector: forward, twenty-five degrees off horizontal. Per notebook page nine.

The eye held position. Six seconds. It rotated counter-clockwise, then resolved.

Bearing: north-by-north-east.

I changed carriage. I threw again.

Bearing: north-by-north-east.

The carriage faces south.

The eye is reading a direction. The train is not in a direction.

I have stopped throwing.

- S. Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 5 variant B (eye of ender, will not fly)
pending.variant:
The eye of ender will not fly.

I threw it forty-seven times. Forward. Up. Sideways. Out the door.

The eye hovers. It spins. It does not pick a direction. Halric wrote that an eye always picks. The eye, page nine: "the only honest thing in a stronghold approach."

It is being honest. There is no stronghold.

I sat with the eye in my hand. It was warm. It is not warm now.

I do not know which way I am going.

- Soll

pending.label: Soll Letter 6 variant A (closed notebook, Soll Marra)
pending.variant:
I have closed the notebook.

The cover is brown leather. There are names inside the front board. I did not look at them.

I am writing on the back page. Halric did not write here.

I am nineteen. I have never seen a dragon. I do not want to kill her.

I am going to keep walking until I find a window with the right kind of light. Then I am going to sit.

Soll Marra

pending.label: Soll Letter 6 variant B (closed notebook, Soll)
pending.variant:
I closed the notebook.

The back page is empty. I sat with it open for a long time.

Halric is not in the back page. Halric is in the front pages. I have read them too many times.

I am nineteen. I have never seen a dragon. I do not want to kill her.

I do not know where this train is going. I think that is alright.

I am going to find a window.

Soll

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant a (chest, three survival tips)
pending.variant:
If you're reading this, you found my chest. Take what's useful.

Three things I wish someone had told me:
1. The slimes don't drop loot worth the swing if you're at half hearts. Walk past.
2. Pots aren't always empty. They aren't always full either. Break them all anyway.
3. Don't sleep on the train. I don't know what happens. I just know I haven't met anyone who has.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant b (trade emeralds for food)
pending.variant: Trade emeralds for food before you trade them for anything else. I learned that one late.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant c (carriage count)
pending.variant:
I've been keeping a count of carriages I've cleared. I'm not going to tell you the number, because the number doesn't matter and it'll only depress you.

Just keep going. The one after the bad one is usually fine.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant d (the same librarian)
pending.variant: If the librarian seems too friendly, he's the same librarian as the last one. They all are. I have no proof of this. I just know it.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant e (built the chest from a barrel)
pending.variant:
This chest used to be a barrel. I broke it down in the dining car and put it back together as a chest because I needed somewhere to leave the things I couldn't carry.

That sentence is tidier than the six hours it actually took. If the lid still closes properly, please appreciate it for a moment before you take everything.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant f (found chest empty, adopted it)
pending.variant:
Found this empty in a corner. Decided it deserved to be useful.

I'm not the first one who put something in it, and I won't be the last. We don't talk to each other, us adventurers, but we leave things in the same boxes. That counts as a kind of talking, I think.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant g (everything in here is on purpose)
pending.variant:
Everything in here is on purpose.

I picked the items I think you'll need most, based on what I needed most. I'm probably wrong about half of them. But I tried.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant h (hidden chest, something empties them)
pending.variant:
I hid this one.

The chests I left in the open kept getting emptied by something I never saw. Don't tell me what you think it was. I don't want to know.

Take what you want. Replace it with something useful if you can.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant i (chain of notes in one chest)
pending.variant:
There was already a book in this chest when I found it. I read it. It was someone else's notes for the next adventurer. I'm leaving it in here too.

You might be holding mine. You might be holding theirs. I can't actually remember which one I'm writing in right now.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant j (terse claim)
pending.variant:
This is my chest. Took me three carriages to find the planks.

Take whatever you need. Try not to take all of it.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k1 (books burn — the fact)
pending.variant:
You'll read this once. Just so you know.

Whatever book you pick up here, however carefully you treat it — once you've read it, it burns. Pages first, then the binding, then the smell of paper, in that order.

I've gotten used to it. I don't promise you will.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k2 (books burn — theory of redundancy)
pending.variant:
My theory on the burning books — and you'll have to forgive me, I've only got the one — is that the train doesn't like being remembered.

You read it. You remember what it said. The book becomes redundant. The train collects redundant things. That's all this is.

I have no evidence for any of that. But it sounds right when I say it out loud.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k3 (books burn — grief over unfinished)
pending.variant:
There's a book I read four carriages back. It burned in my hands before I could finish the last page.

I think about it more than I should. Not because the words were important. Because I'll never know what the last words were.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k4 (books burn — meta closure)
pending.variant:
I write these knowing they'll burn after you read them.

That's the deal. I'm okay with it. If a thing has to be temporary to exist at all, then temporary is the form it takes.

Just — read it. Then let it go.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant l1 (tunnels good for hiding chests)
pending.variant:
Tunnels are good for chests.

Nobody slows down in a tunnel. The light from your lantern moves with you and everything outside that lantern is decided afterwards. Push a chest into a corner of one and most people won't notice it on the way through.

I noticed. So now you've noticed.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant l2 (chests beside tunnel tracks — tools)
pending.variant:
Sometimes there are chests on the side of the tracks in the tunnels. Not on the train. Tucked against the tunnel wall, where the train doesn't reach.

They almost never have anything I'd call good. Pickaxes mostly. Spare rails. Shovels with the handles worn smooth.

Somebody built these tunnels. Somebody is still maintaining them. I try not to think about who.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant m (ice carriage chest)
pending.variant:
This was an ice carriage. Whole car full of it, packed solid. I chipped this chest out with the pickaxe end of an axe I shouldn't have been using like that.

There's water under the floor now. I'm leaving before someone asks me about it.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant n (mud-buried chest)
pending.variant:
Found a chest half-buried in mud. Couldn't tell if someone put it there or if the mud rose up around it.

Both options bothered me equally so I stopped thinking about it and just dug.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant o (moss-covered chests)
pending.variant:
The moss carriages are the worst for finding chests. The moss is patient. It grows over hinges, over latches, sometimes over the whole lid until the chest looks like a green stone.

If you see a green stone in a moss carriage that isn't quite the right shape — that's a chest. Trust me. Or don't. You'll trip over the next one anyway.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant p (thin hallway, look up)
pending.variant:
Some carriages are built thin. Hallway-thin. Just enough room to walk and nothing else.

Look up. There's almost always a space above. Hatches, false ceilings, a foot or two of crawl. Half of what I've found on this train was found by looking up in a place that didn't seem to be hiding anything.

The other half was found by tripping.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant q (finding others' notes by campfires)
pending.variant:
I've started finding other people's notes.

Not many. Maybe one every few hours, or every few carriages — the distance between them is never regular. They turn up at campfires. Almost always at campfires. Like the writer needed to warm up before they could think clearly.

I'd say it makes me feel less alone. It doesn't. It just makes me feel like I'm slightly behind.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant r (campfires heal — that's why)
pending.variant:
Always by a campfire. Always.

The campfires here heal you — I assume you know that by now, or you wouldn't have made it this far. So the writer is stopping where it's safe to think.

I do the same now. I write these by campfires too. That's why you found this one.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant s (might be following someone dead)
pending.variant:
I might be following someone who's already dead.

I keep finding their notes by campfires, but I don't know if they're still ahead of me or if I'm just walking through a trail of things they left a long time ago.

The newest note I've found doesn't have a date. None of them do.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant t (someone is following me too)
pending.variant:
I wonder if someone is following my notes the way I'm following theirs.

If you are — and I have no real reason to think you are not — hello. The campfire's a good place to read this. Wait until the fire's settled. Don't rush.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k5 (books burn — the smell)
pending.variant:
You'll learn the smell.

It's not the smell of a campfire. It's not the smell of wood. It's the smell of words evaporating, if you'll let me get poetic for a second.

After a while you stop noticing. Then you smell it on yourself and realise you've been carrying it for days.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k6 (books burn — slow read)
pending.variant:
I read the next one as slowly as I could. One word at a time, mouthing each one.

It still burned at the end. But it burned more slowly, in a way that felt — and I am almost certain about this — almost grateful.

I don't know what to do with that information.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k7 (books burn — tried not reading)
pending.variant:
I found a book and didn't open it.

I carried it for two days, in my pack, and every time I thought about it I felt a kind of itch. Not on my skin. Inside the thought.

On day three I read it. I had to. I don't know if the book made me, or if I made me. I suspect they were the same thing by that point.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k8 (books burn — afterimage)
pending.variant:
There's a moment, right after a book finishes burning, where you can still see the page. Not on the floor. In your eye. Like a window has been closed and your eye remembers the shape of the light.

If you blink, you lose it. If you don't blink, your eyes water. Either way it's gone in under a minute.

I have started reading the last page twice.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant k9 (books burn — read aloud to campfire)
pending.variant:
I tried reading one to a campfire. Out loud.

The book still burned at the end, but I think the campfire heard. The flame leaned a different way for a moment. Maybe I imagined it.

I have nothing else to base this on. Try it. Tell me what you see.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant u (knowing their hand, no names)
pending.variant:
I know their handwriting now. I knew it before I knew their name. Then I realised they hadn't given me a name in any of the notes I've found.

That bothered me for a day. Then I realised I've never given anyone mine either.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant v (almost catching them)
pending.variant:
Found one tonight that still had the warm-paper smell. Like it had been written in the last hour.

I sat by the same campfire and waited. Nothing came. I'm not sure what I expected.

Eventually I wrote this and left, in the hope that whoever I'm chasing might do the same thing someday and find me by accident instead of effort.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant w (trail goes cold)
pending.variant:
There are no more.

Whatever they were leaving, this was the last one. I've checked the next three campfires. Nothing.

Either they made it out — and I don't believe in that — or this is where they stopped. I'm taking the last note with me. It deserves a witness.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant x (trail picks back up)
pending.variant:
I thought I'd lost the trail. Two days, no notes.

Then today, by a campfire I almost didn't bother stopping at, there it was. Same handwriting. Same way of crossing the sevens.

They're alive. Or someone is. I'm not asking too many questions about which.

pending.label: Adventurer's Field Notes - variant y (disagreement)
pending.variant:
I disagree with the one who wrote before me.

They think the slimes are worth fighting. They are not. I've left a note next to theirs saying so. Whoever finds this last gets to decide.

Disagreement is a kind of company too, I think.

pending.label: Tally - variant a (kill log days 1-?)
pending.variant:
Day 1 — 3 slimes. 1 zombie. Found bread.
Day 4 — Lost count of slimes. Found a sword. Better than mine.
Day 12 — Stopped writing days.
Day ? — Found another one of these books. Wasn't mine. Handwriting almost was.

pending.label: Tally - variant b (kills today: too many)
pending.variant:
Kills today: too many.
Kills total: don't ask.
Deaths: one. So far. Mine.

pending.label: For the Next One - variant a (letter on the body)
pending.variant:
If you find this on me, I'm sorry about the smell.

I came in through a door I can't describe anymore. I think I was looking for something specific. I don't remember what. I remember being sure, and that's the part that bothers me — that being sure can fade so completely.

Take the sword if its still here. It's not enchanted but it swings true and I sharpened it last night, which apparently was a waste of effort but at least it's a gift now.

Don't follow my route. There's nothing useful behind me. Everything I found, I'm wearing.

Whatever you came in looking for — write it down. Now. Before the train decides you didn't.

pending.label: Wren Halloway - 'Rebuttals' (Letters 1-5 variant A, whole story drops at once)

letters.label: Letter One
variants:
Faulthurst.
I read your books.
All of them.


You wrote:
"we trust our eyes
in a place where we
are fools to trust
any of our senses."


Cute.


It's procedural.
The seed changes between rooms.
That's not your senses failing.
That's a bad loop.


— Wren

letters.label: Letter Two
variants:
Faulthurst.
Second letter.
I'm not done.


You wrote:
"I find you
fascinating.
Your differences.
The subtle ways
you differentiate
yourselves without
even trying."


Bored.


You've been here
too long.
You don't find us
"fascinating."
You find us new.
That's not the same.


A Skeptic, Wren

letters.label: Letter Three
variants:
Faulthurst.
The sausage one.
Yes. That one.


You wrote:
"a sausage? And only
one? Who made it?
It was one of a kind."


Statistics.


I have been counting.
Pork keeps coming up.
Beef keeps coming up.
The sausage came up
once. That is not
a miracle, Faulthurst.
That is the pattern.
The one in ten thousand
is what makes the
other nine thousand
look ordinary.


— Wren

letters.label: Letter Four
variants:
Faulthurst.
The "come back" one.
I have notes.


You wrote:
"If you don't, try
to come back! I've
seen it happen with
others! How they do
it, I haven't the
foggiest."


Easy.


You wake up because
the world resets to
where you stood.
You don't return.
The world rewinds.
That is the answer.


Except.
I remember the cold.
The world rewound.
I did not.
Actually.

letters.label: Letter Five
variants:
Faulthurst.
Last one.
I think.


You wrote:
"It's a pleasure
beyond pleasures
to lay witness
to your adventure."


Pleasure.


You meant that.


Damn.

pending.commit_message: content(narrative): add Wren Halloway 'Rebuttals' story (Letters 1-5, variant A only)

pending.label: Wren - Letter 1 variant B (the weight of an infinite train)
pending.variant:
Faulthurst.
I'm working through your books.
This one's about weight.


You wrote:
"What you ever
considered the weight
of an infinite train?
Because each of these
blocks would weight
something constant."


And.


Nothing weighs anything.
The track only exists
where you can see it.
There is no infinite train.
There is one carriage at a time.


— W.

pending.label: Wren - Letter 2 variant B ('soon')
pending.variant:
Faulthurst.
Same author.
Different book.
Same problem.


You wrote:
"The books will be
opening soon."


Soon.


Months ago.
And before that.
There is no "soon."
There is now.
You are stalling.


— Wren

pending.label: Wren - Letter 4 variant B (days are not days outside)
pending.variant:
Faulthurst.
You wrote about time.
Of course you did.


You wrote:
"I know that time
isn't supposed
to move the way it
does in the dungeon
train. I know that
somehow."


Counted.


I have counted.
Days last about
twenty minutes.
Nights are shorter.
That is the clock.
That is the answer.


Except.
Days here are not
days outside.
I know that.
Same as you do.
Actually.

pending.label: Wren - Letter 5 variant B (the refusal — hollow-by-exit)
pending.variant:
Faulthurst.


I am not writing
another one.


That is the rebuttal.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant a (sticky chest hinges)
pending.variant:
Every chest hinge
on this train
is slightly sticky.

Not enough to be broken.
Enough to notice.
I checked twelve carriages.
It is consistent across them.

A stuck hinge can be oiled.
A working hinge can be ignored.
This one cannot be either.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant b (slime residue on boots)
pending.variant:
Slime leaves a film
that does not wash off.
I have tried.

Hot water, sand, dry rags.
The film is still there.
My boots squeak now.
They did not used to squeak.

There are no new boots
on the train.
I have looked.
The slimes are still there.
I step in them every morning.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant c (lanterns and candles underfoot)
pending.variant:
Lanterns on the floor.
Candles in doorways.
Always at ankle height.

Whoever placed them
did not walk back through.
I have tripped sixteen times
that I will admit to.

I memorise where they are.
The carriages repeat
in new arrangements.
The lanterns move with them.
The bruises do not.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant d (someone moves the lanterns)
pending.variant:
In carriage 213,
the lanterns are not
where I left them.

The one by the door
has moved to the other side.
The two at the back
are a hand's width closer.
One is missing.
A different one is here.

I waited three nights.
Nothing moved.
On the fourth I slept.
They moved.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant e (snorers, bench-by-bench audit)
pending.variant:
Fifteen sleeping cars
I have surveyed.
I will sleep in four.

Car 2341, bench three:
snores with a whistle.
Car 6792, bench eight:
snores with a cough.
Car 9847, bench one:
snores the same tune
every night.

I sleep in the other four.
Some nights a new snorer
finds my bench.
Some nights an old one
moves to mine.
The system is failing.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant f (tough mobs, lousy loot)
pending.variant:
A thing in iron armour.
It dropped a bone.

Three crossbowmen.
Between them, one emerald.
A thing in chainmail.
It dropped a potato.

The loot does not justify
the swing of the sword.
I keep swinging.

pending.label: Complaint Log - variant g (the dirt cart, landing-page variant)
pending.variant:
Once, by accident,
I dug out a dirt cart
and found a chest inside.

I have dug out
forty-three dirt carts since.
I have used shovels.
I have used my hands.
I have dug them slowly.
I have dug them fast.
They have all been empty.

I have tried not looking.
I have tried looking away.
I have tried digging blind.
I have tried whistling.
I have tried silence.
I have tried thinking of soup.
I have tried thinking of nothing.
The train knows
when I am looking.

pending.label: Glossary: THE PRY-PAUSE
pending.variant:
THE PRY-PAUSE:
-
The half-second
before a chest opens.
The breath you hold
without meaning to.


A long pry-pause is
the kind we speak of —
the kind of a chest
worth waiting on.
Of shorter ones, nothing.

pending.label: Glossary: THE LONG LEAN
pending.variant:
THE LONG LEAN:
-
What is said to happen
on a curve —
the whole carriage
tilts as one.


Those who have felt one
speak of it unprompted.
The rest of us
trust the telling.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): glossary entry THE LONG LEAN

pending.label: Glossary: A REBOARDER
pending.variant:
A REBOARDER:
-
A passenger who claims
they got off the train
and came back.


Most are lying.
The ones who aren't
won't bring it up.
You learn to listen
for the silence.

pending.label: Glossary: RETURNED AROUND
pending.variant:
RETURNED AROUND:
-
The next carriage
feels like one
you'd already crossed.
The certainty the train
must have turned.


No-one sees it happen.
You feel it after.
You ask the next
passenger if they did.
They never have.

pending.label: Glossary: WOBBLY TIME
pending.variant:
WOBBLY TIME:
-
Time that won't
hold still long enough
to be measured.
The only kind here.


We try to count it.
Two passengers
comparing notes
will never agree
on how long.

pending.label: Glossary: A WARNING-DIM
pending.variant:
A WARNING-DIM:
-
When the lanterns
dim twice in a row.
No-one decided this
meant something —
it just does.


You'll see passengers
quicken their step
without speaking.
Nothing makes them.
Most of us do anyway.

pending.label: Glossary: A SILENT FRIEND
pending.variant:
A SILENT FRIEND:
-
A passenger who greets
you with a nod
and nothing more.
Never unkind.
Just wordless.


Over enough carriages
you come to tell
one nod from another.
That is friendship,
of a kind.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): glossary entry A SILENT FRIEND

pending.label: Glossary: A BIOME-SLIP
pending.variant:
A BIOME-SLIP:
-
When the world outside
changes. Desert to
forest. Forest to ice.
No-one quite sees
it happen.


The slip happens
in the looking-away.
The world waits
for you to turn
before it changes.

pending.label: Glossary: TO SETTLE-IN
pending.variant:
TO SETTLE-IN:
-
To stay in a carriage
long enough
that you stop counting
how long.


You can tell who has.
They have stopped
asking how long.
The question
no longer fits.

pending.label: Glossary: THE UNSTALE
pending.variant:
THE UNSTALE:
-
Bread you have carried
for so long
that it should be stale.
It isn't.
It never is.


Some refuse to eat it
once they notice.
The rest of us
eat it anyway.

pending.label: Glossary: THE DENAMED
pending.variant:
THE DENAMED:
-
A passenger who can
no longer recall
their own name.
The carriages take it
quietly, over time.


You can sit beside one
and not know.
They have no name
to give you.

pending.label: Glossary: PASSING-WORDS
pending.variant:
PASSING-WORDS:
-
What you say
to a passenger
you'll never see again.
They don't have
to mean anything.


A nod will do.
A word about weather.
The point is the saying,
not the said.

pending.label: Glossary: AN OLD-HOLD
pending.variant:
AN OLD-HOLD:
-
An item you have kept
so long
you no longer remember
bringing it
onto the train.


You forget when
it was new to you.
You forget
it was ever
not yours.

pending.label: Glossary: THE THRESHOLD-HOLD
pending.variant:
THE THRESHOLD-HOLD:
-
The half-second pause
before you step
from one carriage
into the next.


We do it without
deciding.
Something in us
checks the next
carriage is still there.

pending.label: Glossary: OFFLOADING
pending.variant:
OFFLOADING:
-
The slow ritual
of sitting with
your things
and deciding
what to drop.


You always know
which thing to drop.
Sorting just lets
the hands catch up.

pending.label: Glossary: A SHARE-OUT
pending.variant:
A SHARE-OUT:
-
An item you set down
in a chest, on purpose,
for the next person
who opens it.


You leave them
for whoever opens
the chest next.
You don't know who.
You hope.

pending.label: Glossary: A CARRY-DOWN
pending.variant:
A CARRY-DOWN:
-
A thing left in a chest
on purpose
by whoever opened it
before you.
Now yours.


You won't meet them.
You won't know who.
You carry the thing
forward, and maybe
leave one back.

pending.label: Glossary: TWO HANDS BOTH FULL
pending.variant:
TWO HANDS BOTH FULL:
-
The moment you find
two things you want,
and only one
will leave with you.


The lesser-wanted
becomes a share-out.
You feel both
choices in your hands
for a while after.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): glossary entry TWO HANDS BOTH FULL

pending.label: Glossary: A POCKET-DARLING
pending.variant:
A POCKET-DARLING:
-
An item you keep
for its name,
its trim, its words.
You will never use it.
You will never drop it.


Everyone has one.
Most of us
have several.
We rarely admit
how many.

pending.label: Glossary: FIRST-GLINT
pending.variant:
FIRST-GLINT:
-
The first half-second
of seeing something
in a chest
that you have not seen
before.


Before you know
what it is.
Before your hand
reaches.
A small light
behind the eyes.

pending.label: Glossary: A GOLDEN-STRIKE
pending.variant:
A GOLDEN-STRIKE:
-
The moment you know
the item is one
you have never owned.
The first time
in the holding.


You don't shout.
You don't speak.
You sit with it.
The whole carriage
feels brighter than
just before.

pending.label: Glossary: A LOOK-PAST
pending.variant:
A LOOK-PAST:
-
An item whose name
you decide not
to read twice.
The kind nobody
should have written.


You look past the name.
You set the item back.
Later, you try not to
recall what it said.
You pray to forget.

pending.label: Glossary: A QUICK-SHELVE
pending.variant:
A QUICK-SHELVE:
-
An item whose
inscription reads
like a warning
not meant for you.


You shelve it fast.
You don't finish
the line.
You move on.
You don't look back.

pending.label: Glossary: AN INWARD-WHY
pending.variant:
AN INWARD-WHY:
-
A question you ask
yourself
that you have never
asked yourself
before.


Always the same shape.
Never the same answer.
Each carriage asks them
a little louder.

pending.label: Glossary: THE WAKING-DOUBT
pending.variant:
THE WAKING-DOUBT:
-
The question
that arrives
some carriages in.
Is any of this
actually here?


You touch the wall.
The wall is there.
This does not settle
the question.

pending.label: Glossary: A QUESTIONEER
pending.variant:
A QUESTIONEER:
-
A passenger who has
stopped finding answers
and started finding
questions everywhere.


The chair, a question.
The lantern, the same.
You become a question
they ask without
opening their mouth.

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant a (Zombie common)
pending.variant:
The zombie walks. It does not breathe. It does not warm. The body is here. The body is not what is making it walk.

Medicine asks how a body fails. The zombie offers nothing for medicine to take hold of — there is no failure. There is only persistence.

The question is not medical. It is structural. What is this thing? How does it hold together? Those are questions for someone who is not a naturalist.

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant b (Zombie with a shovel)
pending.variant:
The zombie carries a shovel. The grip is not random. The fingers are arranged as fingers are arranged when there is something to dig.

Three theories present themselves. The shovel was buried with this specimen. The shovel was found by this specimen. Or it is not, in any meaningful sense, a shovel.

None of the theories satisfy. The shovel is not used. It is held. The holding is the thing.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): bestiary fragments variant b (zombie with shovel)

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant c (Zombie Villager)
pending.variant:
The zombie villager wears its profession. This one wears the apron of a butcher. Whatever this one was, it was that.

The clothes have not been chosen. They are simply still there. A wardrobe outlasting the person who chose it.

I do not know if the villager remains. I do not know if any of them remains. I know the clothes do.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): bestiary fragments variant c (zombie villager)

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant d (Baby Zombie)
pending.variant:
There are smaller zombies. The mass is roughly half. The speed is greater. This is not the usual arrangement.

The sound is also wrong. The larger ones groan low. These are higher. Closer to something one should not have to hear.

They do not shamble. They run. They run faster than a person can run, and they do not stop. I have learned this on foot.

There is a word for a smaller form of a creature. I will not use it. The implication is not one I am prepared to follow.
pending.commit_message: content(narrative): bestiary fragments variant d (baby zombie)

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant e (Skeleton)
pending.variant:
The skeleton is bone alone. No skin, no muscle, no organ. The structure that supports a body — and no body.

It holds a bow. The bow is drawn. There is no muscle to draw it. There is nothing in the chest to brace against. The bow is drawn.

The literature does not account for this. A skeleton is what remains when the body is gone — the end of inquiry. But this skeleton walks. There is nothing here to dissect. The dissection has already been done.

Not by me.

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant f (on the question of pain)
pending.variant:
I would like to believe these specimens do not feel. The literature, such as it is, provides nothing.

The zombies persist regardless of injury. The skeletons do not flinch. This may mean they do not feel. Or it may mean they feel and continue regardless.

I have considered both. I have decided nothing. The deciding costs more than I have to give.

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant g (marginal observation, train-unawareness)
pending.variant:
The fauna do not appear to know they are on a train. The carriage shifts. They do not brace. The window shows passing country. They do not look.

They behave as if they were anywhere. The train is not a context they recognise. It is, to them, a room.

They have been here as long as I have. They have not, in that time, noticed where here is.

pending.label: Bestiary Fragments - variant h (to whoever finds this)
pending.variant:
If you are reading this notebook, I am not. Or I have set it down and not returned.

If you came to the train to study the fauna — do not. The methods of natural history do not apply. The specimens are not specimens. They are something the methods do not have a name for — and the methods cost more than they pay.

I am sorry, whoever you are. I did not know I was writing for someone to warn.
